coredata.dc:title: An Experimental Investigation into Plate-to-Roll Patterning with Solid-State Superionic Stamping
coredata.prism:issueName: 47th SME North American Manufacturing Research Conference, NAMRC 47, Pennsylvania, USA.
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Ping-Ju
Qian, Boqiang
Barna, Shama F.
Mensing, Glennys
Ferreira, Placid
coredata.dc:description: Metallic nanostructures play an important role in micro and nanotechnology, from interconnects in electronics, electrodes in chemical sensors, batteries, plasmonic waveguides, structural color generation in sub-wavelength optics and catalysts in micro-reactor surfaces. Silver, of all the elements, possesses the highest electrical conductivity and (as a result) unique interaction with visible light when patterned at the nanoscale, making it ideal for electronics, optics and chemical sensing applications. Solid-State Superionic Stamping (S4) is an electrochemical nano-imprinting process that is capable of directly patterning silver nanostructures. In this paper, we demonstrate a simple method to scale up S4 patterning of silver using a plate-to-roll format to explore the potential of the S4 technology in a roll-to-roll configuration for high-volume manufacturing. In this format, a roller with a silver-coated polymer film wrapped around it is rolled without slip over a planar, patterned (x)AgI(1-x)AgPO3, x=0.5, stamp while creating an electrochemical cell with the silver film as the anode. This format allows us to explore the advantages of patterning with a moving line contact (as opposed to the large stationary surface contact in the imprinting/stamping format) between the stamp and roller, but without the added complexity of having to make a patterned electrolyte roller. We demonstrate the making of 6x10 mm2 patterned silver surfaces with arrays of 200 nm holes or pillars having an aspect ratio of up to 2. The effects of process parameters including rolling speed, contact pressure, patterning voltage and current on the pattern quality and the stamp surface degradation are studied.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanopatterning
Superionic Conductors
Nanoimprinting
Plate-to-roll Patterning
Electrochemistry

xocs:title-editors-group.ce:title: 47th SME North American Manufacturing Research Conference, NAMRC 47, Pennsylvania, USA.

xocs:cp-license-lines.xocs:cp-license-line: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license.

xocs:doc.xocs:rawtext: ScienceDirect Available online at www.sciencedirect.comAvailable online at www.sciencedirect.com ScienceDirectÂ Procedia Manufacturing 00 (2017) 000â€“000 www.elsevier.com/locate/procedia * Paulo Afonso. Tel.: +351 253 510 761; fax: +351 253 604 741 E-mail address: psafonso@dps.uminho.pt 2351-9789 Â© 2017 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V. Peer-review under responsibility of the scientific committee of the Manufacturing Engineering Society International Conference 2017. Manufacturing Engineering Society International Conference 2017, MESIC 2017, 28-30 June 2017, Vigo (Pontevedra), Spain Costing models for capacity optimization in Industry 4.0: Trade-off between used capacity and operational efficiency A. Santanaa, P. Afonsoa,*, A. Zaninb, R. Wernkeb a University of Minho, 4800-058 GuimarÃ£es, Portugal bUnochapecÃ³, 89809-000 ChapecÃ³, SC, Brazil Abstract Under the concept of "Industry 4.0", production processes will be pushed to be increasingly interconnected, information based on a real time basis and, necessarily, much more efficient. In this context, capacity optimization goes beyond the traditional aim of capacity maximization, contributing also for organizationâ€™s profitability and value. Indeed, lean management and continuous improvement approaches suggest capacity optimization instead of maximization. The study of capacity optimization and costing models is an important research topic that deserves contributions from both the practical and theoretical perspectives. This paper presents and discusses a mathematical model for capacity management based on different costing models (ABC and TDABC). A generic model has been developed and it was used to analyze idle capacity and to design strategies towards the maximization of organizationâ€™s value. The trade-off capacity maximization vs operational efficiency is highlighted and it is shown that capacity optimization might hide operational inefficiency. Â© 2017 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V. Peer-review under responsibility of the scientific committee of the Manufacturing Engineering Society International Conference 2017. Keywords: Cost Models; ABC; TDABC; Capacity Management; Idle Capacity; Operational Efficiency 1. Introduction The cost of idle capacity is a fundamental information for companies and their management of extreme importance in modern production systems. In general, it is defined as unused capacity or production potential and can be measured in several ways: tons of production, available hours of manufacturing, etc. The management of the idle capacity Procedia Manufacturing 34 (2019) 424â€“431 2351-9789 Â© 2019 The Authors. Published by Elsevi r B.V. This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/3.0/) Peer-review under responsibility of the Scientific Committee of NAMRI/SME. 10.1016/j.promfg.2019.06.187 10.1016/j.promfg.2019.06.187 2351-9789 Â© 2019 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V. This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/3.0/) Peer-review under esponsibility of the Scientific Committee of NAMRI/SME. 47th SME North American Manufacturing Research Conference, Penn State Behrend Erie, Pennsylvania, 2019 Available online at www.sciencedirect.com ScienceDire t Procedia Manufacturing 00 (2019) 000â€“000 www.elsevier.com/locate/procedia 2351-9789 Â© 2019 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V. This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/3.0/) Peer-review under responsibility of the Scientific Committee of NAMRI/SME. 47th SME North American Manufacturing Research Conference, NAMRC 47, Pennsylvania, USA An Experimental Investigation into Plate-to-Roll Patterning with Solid- State Superionic Stamping Ping-Ju Chena, Boqiang Qiana, Shama F. Barnaa, Glennys Mensinga, Placid Ferreiraa,* aUniversity of Illinois at Urbana-Champaign, 1206 W Green St, Urbana, IL and 61801, USA bUniversity of Illinois at Urbana-Champaign, 1206 W Green St, Urbana, IL and 61801, USA * Corresponding author. Tel.: 1-217-333-0639; fax: +1-217-244-6534. E-mail address: pferreir@illinois.edu Abstract Metallic nanostructures play an important role in micro and nanotechnology, from interconnects in electronics, electrodes in chemical sensors, batteries, plasmonic waveguides, structural color generation in sub-wavelength optics and catalysts in micro- reactor surfaces. Silver, of all the elements, possesses the highest electrical conductivity and (as a result) unique interaction with visible light when patterned at the nanoscale, making it ideal for electronics, optics and chemical sensing applications. Solid-State Superionic Stamping (S4) is an electrochemical nano-imprinting process that is capable of directly patterning silver nanostructures. In this paper, we demonstrate a simple method to scale up S4 patterning of silver using a plate-to-roll format to explore the potential of th S4 technology in a roll-to-roll configuration for high-volume manufacturing. In this format, a roller with a silver-coated polymer film wrapped around it is rolled without slip ove a planar, patterned (x)AgI(1-x)AgPO3, x=0.5, stamp while creating a electrochemical cell with the silver film as the anode. This format allows us to explore the advantages of patterning with a moving line contact (as opposed to the large stationary surface contact in the imprinting/stamping format) between the stamp and roller, but without the added complexity of having to make a patterned electrolyte roller. We demonstrate the making of 6x10 mm2 patterned silver surfaces with arrays of 200 nm holes or pillars having an aspect ratio of up to 2. The effects of process parameters including r lling speed, contact pressure, patterning voltage and current on the tt rn quality and the stamp surface degradation are studied. Â© 2019 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V. This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/3.0/) Peer-review under responsibility of the Scientific Committee of NAMRI/SME. Keywords: Nanopatterning; Superionic Conductors; Nano mprinting; Plate-to-roll Patterning; Electrochemistry 1. Introduction The proper functioning of metallic nanostructures in novel applications such as plasmonic bio-sensing, structural colours, metamaterials, etc., is highly dependent on their sizes, shapes and the relative location. This has motivated an upsurge in research on man facturing methods that achieve this co trol of the m tal geometry at nanoscale, while at the same time permitting affordable scale-up to large-area patterning. Conventional (lithography based) ap roaches to fabricating metallic patterns are indirect, time-consuming and costly. For example, micro- or nanoscale features are first photolithographically patterned in a photoresist polymer to function as a sacrificial mold. Metal is then deposited, using evaporation or sputtering. Finally, a lift-off step removes the polymer and leaves the metal pattern behind. Conventional photolithography allows micro-scale patterning over an entire wafer, resulting in high overall hroughput. However, the feature resolution is limited by both the wavel ngth of the light used and the thickness of the resist [1]. Electron beam lithography and focused ion beam lithography are capable of writing arbitrary shapes of nanoscale features with resolutions Available online at www.sciencedirect.com Sci nceDirect Procedia Manufacturing 00 (2019) 000â€“000 www.elsevier.com/locate/procedia 2351-9789 Â© 2019 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V. This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/3.0/) Peer-review under responsibility of the Scientific Committee of NAMRI/SME. 47th SME North A erican Manufacturing Research Conference, NAMRC 47, Pennsylvania, USA An Experimental Investigation into Plate-to-Roll Patterning with Solid- State Superionic Stamping Ping-Ju Chena, Boqiang Qiana, Shama F. Barnaa, Glennys Mensinga, Placid Ferreiraa,* aUniversity of Illinois at Urbana-Champaign, 1206 W Green St, Urbana, IL and 61801, USA bUniversity of Illinois at Urbana-Champaign, 1206 W Green St, Urbana, IL and 61801, USA * Corresponding author. Tel.: 1-217-333-0639; fax: +1-217-244-6534. E-mail address: pferreir@illinois.edu Abstract Metallic nanostructures play an important role in micro and nanotechnology, from interconnects in electronics, electrodes in chemical sensors, batteries, plasmonic waveguides, structural color generation in sub-wavelength optics and catalysts in micro- reactor surfaces. Silver, of all the elements, possesses the highest electrical conductivity and (as a result) unique interaction with visible light when patterned at the nanoscale, making it ideal for electronics, optics and chemical sensing applications. Solid-State Superionic Stamping (S4) is an electrochemical nano-imprinting process that is capable of directly patterning silver nanostructures. In this paper, we demonstrate a simple method to scale up S4 patterning of silver using a plate-to-roll format to explore the potential of the S4 technology in a roll-to-roll configuration for high-volume manufacturing. In this format, a roller with a silver-coated polymer film wrapped around it is rolled without slip over a planar, patterned (x)AgI(1-x)AgPO3, x=0.5, stamp while creating an electrochemical cell with the silver film as the anode. This format allows us to explore the advantages of atterning with a moving line contact (as opposed to the large statio ary surface contact in the imprinting/ tamping format) between the stamp and roller, but without the added complexity of having to make a patterned electrolyte roller. We demonstrate the making of 6x10 mm2 patterned silver surfaces with arrays of 200 nm holes or pillars having an aspect ratio of up to 2. The effects of process parameters including rolling speed, contact pressure, patterning voltage and current on the pattern quality and the stamp surface degradation are studied. Â© 2019 he Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V. This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/3.0/) Peer-review under responsibility of the Scientific Committee of NAMRI/SME. Keywords: Nanopatterning; Superionic Conductors; Nanoimprinting; Plate-to-roll Patterning; Electrochemistry 1. Introduction The proper functioning of metallic nanostructures in novel applications such as plasmonic bio-sensing, structural colours, metamaterials, etc., is highly dependent on their sizes, shapes and the relative location. This has motivated an upsurge in research on manufacturing methods that achieve this control of the metal geometry at nanoscale, while at the same time permitting affordable scale-up to large-area patterning. Conventional (lithography based) approaches to fabricating metallic patterns are indirect, time-consuming and costly. For example, micro- or nanoscale features are first photolithographically patterned in a photoresist polymer to function as a sacrificial mold. Metal is then deposited, using evaporation or sputtering. Finally, a lift-off step removes the polymer and leaves the metal pattern behind. Conventional photolithography allows micro-scale patterning over an entire wafer, resulting in high overall throughput. However, the feature resolution is limited by both the wavelength of the light used and the thickness of the resist [1]. Electron beam lithography and focused ion beam lithography are capable of writing arbitrary shapes of nanoscale features with resolutions Available online at www.sciencedirect.com ScienceDirect Procedia Manufacturing 00 (2019) 000â€“000 www.elsevier.com/locate/procedia 2351-9789 Â© 2019 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V. This is an ope acces article under CC BY-N -ND lic nse (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/3.0/) Peer-review under responsibility of the Scientific Committee of NAMRI/SME. 47th SME North American Manufacturing Research Conference, NAMRC 47, Pennsylvania, USA An Experimental Investigation into Plate-to-Roll Patterning with Solid- State Superionic Stamping Ping-Ju Chena, Boqiang Qiana, Shama F. Barnaa, Glennys Mensinga, Placid Ferreiraa,* a bUniversity of Illinois at Urbana-Champaign, 1206 W Green St, Urbana, IL and 61801, USA * Corresponding author. Tel.: 1-217-333-0639; fax: +1-217-244-6534. E-mail address: pferreir@illinois.edu Abstract Metallic nanostructures play an important role in micro and nanotechnology, from interconnects in electronics, electrodes in chemical sensors, batteries, plasmonic waveguides, structural color generation in sub-wavelength optics and catalysts in micro- reactor surfaces. Silver, of all t e elements, possesses the highest electrical conductivity and (as a result) unique interaction with visible light when patterned at the nanoscale, making it ideal for electronics, optics and chemical sensing applications. Solid-State Superionic Stamping (S4) is an electrochemical nano-imprinting process that is capable of directly patterning silver nanostructures. In t is paper, we demonstrate a simple method to scale up S4 patterning of silver using a plate-t -roll format to explore the potential of the S4 technology in a roll-to-roll configuration for high-volume anufacturing. In this format, a roller with a silver-coated p lymer film wrappe ar und it is rolled without slip over a planar, patterned ( )AgI(1-x)AgPO3, x=0.5, stamp while creating an electr chemical cell with the silver film as the anode. This format allows us to explore the advantages of patterning with a moving line contact (as opposed to the large statio ary surface contact in the imprinting/stamping format) between the stamp and roller, but without the added complexit of having to make a patterned electrolyte roller. We demonstrate the making of 6x10 mm2 patterned silver surfaces with arrays of 200 nm holes or pillars having an aspect ratio of up to 2. The effects of process parameters including rolling speed, contact pressure, patterning voltage and current on the pattern quality and the stamp surface degradatio are studi . Â© 2019 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V. This is an open access article under the CC BY-N -ND license (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/3.0/) Peer-review under responsibility of the Scientific Committee of NAMRI/SME. Keywords: Nanopatterning; Superionic Conductors; Nanoimprinting; Plate-to-roll Patterning; Electrochemistry 1. Introduction The proper functioning of metallic nanostructures in novel applications such as plasmonic bio-sensing, structural colours, metamaterials, etc., is highly dependent on their sizes, shapes and the relative location. This has motivated an upsurge in research on m nufacturing methods that achieve this c ntrol f the metal geometry at nanoscale, while at the same time permitting affordable scale-up to large-area patterning. Conventional (lithography based) approaches to fabricating metallic patterns ar indirect, time-consuming and costly. For example, micro- or nanoscale features are first photolithographically patterned in a photoresist polymer to function as a sacrificial mold. Metal is then deposited, using evaporation or sputtering. Finally, a lift-off step removes the polymer and leaves the etal pattern behind. Conventional photolithography allows micro-scale patterning over an entire wafer, resulting in igh overall throughput. However, the feature resolution is limited by both the wavelength of the light used and the thickness of the resist [1]. Electron beam lithography and focused ion beam lithography are capable of writing arbitrary shapes of nanoscale features with resolutions il l li i i i t i . l i . /l t / i t . li l i . . i i ti l li tt :// ti . /li / / . / i i ilit t i ti i itt / . , i it f lli i t i , t, , , i t . l.: ; : . il i illi i . n . . . . . . tt i ; i i t ; i i ti ; l t t ll tt i ; l t i t a o o e h Ping-Ju Chen et al. / Procedia Manufacturing 34 (2019) 424â€“431 425 47th SME North American Manufacturing Research Conference, Penn State Behrend Erie, Pennsylvania, 2019 2 Author name / Procedia Manufacturing 00 (2019) 000â€“000 down to 5 nm [2][3], but both processes are extremely slow, area-limited and expensive because they require the beam to scan the entire pattern in a pixel-by-pixel approach. Less conventional methods, such as nano-transfer printing, use a polydimethyl-siloxane (PDMS) stamp to directly transfer patterns of metallic film on the substrate and are suitable for micrometer-sized features but often require pretreatment of the substrate [4]. More economical but not as flexible, nanosphere lithography can be used for creating triangular periodic patterns of metallic structures by using closely packed polystyrene microspheres as a mask [5]. Another method, interference lithography [6], has the potential to create large-area periodic patterns. The disadvantages of both techniques are the limited geometric shapes of the patterns that can be created and the limited positioning and size control of the nanostructures. Given the increasing use and need for metallic nanostructures in nanotechnology applications, direct manufacturing techniques that provide the flexibility of fabricating metallic nanostructures with the desired geometry over large areas and at speeds and costs that make commercial applications economically feasible are of critical importance. 1.1. Scalable Nano-manufacturing with Solid-State Superionic Stamping (S4) S4 is a facile manufacturing process that eliminates the need for multi-step processing, ultra-high vacuums, special liquid/gas handling or stringent environmental (pressure, temperature, stoichiometry) controls in the nano-patterning of several metals for which efficient solid electrolytes are available [7]â€“[11]. The basic framework of electrochemical patterning processes using solid electrolytes involves an electrolytic cell composed of a cathode and anode, bridged by a superionic conductor. Superionic conductors conduct electricity efficiently (with conductivity greater than 0.1 Ohmâˆ’1 cmâˆ’1) at room temperature and in the solid state by means of a mobile species of ions (typically the cation) [12]. Inserted as a solid electrolyte in an electrolytic cell, they act as the medium through which material is supplied to (for additive processes) or removed from (for subtractive processes) the substrate being processed. A subtractive manufacturing approach based on this scheme is known as Solid-State Superionic Stamping (S4), where Hsu et al. used the solid electrolytes such as Ag2S and Cu2S to etch features into silver and copper films with nanoscale precision [6, 7]. The superionic conductor or solid electrolyte, which is used as the â€˜stampâ€™ in the process, has its surface patterned by focused ion beam milling, so it contacts the metal film (configured as the anode in the electrochemical cell) to be patterned only at areas where metal is to be removed. When an anodic bias is applied to the film to be patterned, metal at the metal-electrolyte interface is anodically dissolved (oxidized into metal ions) and transported through the electrolyte to the cathode (where it is reduced back to metal and deposited). Finally, when the stamp is removed, it leaves behind a pattern on the metal substrate that is complementary to that on its own surface. Ag2S and Cu2S are mixed electrolytes (conduct both electrons and ions) and are therefore inefficient as stamp materials. Further, because they are highly malleable polycrystalline, it is difficult to create nano patterns in them and make large blemish-free flat surfaces with them [7]. Later Jacobs et al demonstrated a significant improvement to the S4 process by silver patterning using a superionic (AgI)x- (AgPO3)(1-x) glass as the stamp material [13]. (AgI)x-(AgPO3)(1- x) provides an excellent balance of desired properties, including chemical stability in ambient conditions, high ionic conductivity at room temperature and good optical transparency (for overlay registration in stamping). Its low glass transition temperature (typically 85Â°C to 110Â°C) facilitates the making of stamps using melt-processing techniques (hot-embossing and molding). The use of (AgI)x- (AgPO3)(1-x) in the S4 process allows patterning of silver with sub-100 nm features over a 25 mm2 area [11]. S4 provides several advantages over other scalable patterning approaches (like nanoimprint lithography) when it comes to patterning metallic films. But, as with all imprinting technologies, it demands exacting flatness and alignment of the stamp. This reduces the size of stamps that can be economically produced and greatly increases the cost of the machinery needed to align the stamp and the substrate before imprinting. Therefore, fabrication of large area (> 5mm in diameter) S4 stamps and subsequent patterning needs to be explored for scaling up this process for manufacturing on an industrial scale. Additionally, roll-to-roll formats offer the advantage of requiring only line contact between the roller stamp and the substrate. This advantage could greatly alleviate the aforementioned difficulties associate with large area contact. In section 2, we explore the fabricating of large area stamps, specifically demonstrating the feasibility of making and imprinting with stamps having diameters of 10mm. In section 3, a route to roll-to-roll S4 patterning is explored, and we conduct experiments with a roll-plate patterning process to demonstrate the feasibility of S4 patterning with a moving line contact. Section 4 draws up conclusions and describes directions for future work. 2. Fabrication of S4 stamp for large area (> 5 mm diameter) patterning As previously mentioned, the AgI-AgPO3 stamps can conveniently be melt processed. However, as the patterned stamp cools to room temperature, the stamp surface can develop a curvature [14] due to many factors including the viscoelasticity of the glass, thermal contraction during solidification and cooling of (AgI)0.5-(AgPO3)0.5, and load induced elastic deformation of the mold. Imprinting, in general, requires that the entire stamp make contact with the film being patterned so that the pattern transfers from the stamp to the film. Thus, with S4, the entire solid electrolyte stamp needs to be in physical contact with the silver film during electrochemical etching. Only the smallest of curvatures (those that can be overcome by the contact pressure) can be tolerated. In an attempt to create a flat stamp, the effects of different process parameters (mold temperature, stamp temperature, imprinting load etc.) on stamp curvature during molding have been previously investigated [14]. While that characterization led to the ability to fabricate stamps with less than 200 nm total curvature across a 5 mm diameter of the stamp, it also stated 426 Ping-Ju Chen et al. / Procedia Manufacturing 34 (2019) 424â€“431 Author name / Procedia Manufacturing 00 (2019) 000â€“000 3 that a 50-120 MPa pressure is needed to compensate any surface curvature for the 5 mm stamp to achieve good contact for the etching process [11]. The difficulty in molding stamps by thermal nanoimprint at larger scales will be the induced stresses due to volumetric thermal contraction from the glass transition temperature, Tg, to the S4 processing temperature, typically at room temperature. This exacerbates the curvature issue for larger stamps. The fabrication of a flat S4 stamp with a diameter larger than 5 mm has not been explored but is essential in order to scale up the patterning process. Figure 1 shows the fabrication process and challenges of S4 patterning for a 10 mm stamp. Figure 1 (a) and (b) shows a schematic of the as-fabricated 10 mm stamp (with an optical image below) and the experimental setup. A 10 mm S4 stamp was inserted into the stamp holder on the S4 machine, and the stamp holder and mold are maintained at temperatures Ts (65 Â°C), and Tm (100 Â°C), respectively. A constant compressive load of 5 lb. is then applied to the stamp-mold interface to bring the (AgI)0.5(AgPO3)0.5 in contact with the mold. The temperatures are selected such that Tm is above the Tg of (AgI)0.5(AgPO3)0.5 (~80 Â°C), and Ts is kept below Tg so that softening and viscoelastic spreading only occurs at the stamp- mold interface. Once the (AgI)0.5(AgPO3)0.5 at the contact interface spreads out to a diameter of 10 mm, the compressive load on the stamp holder is reduced to a 0.5 lb. load to stop further spreading of the glass. To allow the viscoelastic stresses in (AgI)0.5(AgPO3)0.5 to relax, a holding time of 1 minute is maintained before cooling the mold at a rate of 1.5 Â°C/minute. A compressive load of 0.5 lb. is maintained while cooling to ensure contact of the stamp surface with the mold. Once Tm falls below Tg, the (AgI)0.5(AgPO3)0.5 glass at the interface solidifies facilitating demolding, once the applied load is removed and the stamp is retracted from the mold by raising the Z axis stage of the imprinting machine. Figure 1 (c) shows the arrangement of the set up for electrochemical etching using the S4 process. Figure 1 (d) shows the results of patterning on the silver film using the as-fabricated 10 mm diameter stamp. The patterning results show that etching of the silver film occurred only at the circumference of the stamp surface rather than the entire 10 mm diameter area suggesting that the fabricated stamp surface is concave. The concavity of the 10 mm stamp is therefore studied using profilometer measurements of the entire stamp surface prepared at different process parameters (stamp holder temperature, compressive load etc.). Figure 2 (a) shows the profilometer data of the stamp molded against the same polished silicon wafer using different stamp holder temperatures while keeping all the other parameters unchanged. The results show that when the stamp was molded using a stamp holder temperature at 65 Â°C, the stamp surface is concave with a maximum 1.6 Âµm gap at the center. Increasing the stamp holder temperature reduces the concavity of the stamp surface because the temperature gradient across the stamp (from the mold to the stamp holder) is decreased and made more uniform. However, even at a temperature of 75 Â°C, the concavity is only reduced by 300 nm. Further reduction of the stamp surface curvature by increasing the stamp holder temperature is not possible because of its proximity to Tg. Raising the holder temperature any further causes the entire stamp to soften and reflow. Next, the effect of compressive load during the molding process on the stamp surface profiles is explored by varying the compressive load while keeping the stamp holder temperature at 75 Â°C. At temperatures this close to Tg, the increased molding pressures are seen to directly influence the flow rate of the glass. It was observed that when the compressive molding load was increased proportionally to the desired final area of the stamp, the molding time remained constant. Thus, increasing the compressive load for molding a 10 mm diameter stamp to 20 lbf (from 5 lbf for a 5 mm diameter stamp), allowed the deformation phase of the molding process to complete during the allotted 1 minute of holding time. Further, a relaxation time of 3 minutes released the viscoelastic stresses produced during deformation while the mold and the stamp holder were cooled below Tg. Figure 2 (b) shows surface profiles of stamps fabricated by applying different compressive loads while molding with a stamp holder temperature of 75 Â°C. The profile of the stamp surface is seen to transition from concave to convex as the compressive load increases from 5 lbf to 25 lbf. Therefore, a compressive load between 20 lbf to 25 lbf should be applied to produce flat stamps with a circular area of 10 mm diameter. The final molding recipe for 10 mm diameter S4 stamps therefore recommends a compressive load of 22.5 lbf. with stamp holder temperature at 75 Â°C. Figure 3 (a) shows an optical image of the fabricated stamp, and Figure 3 (b) shows successful patterning of silver using the stamp over a circular Figure 1. S4 patterning using a stamp with a diameter of 10 mm. (a) A schematic section of the stamp showing how the glass is held on an Al stamp holder. Image of a S4 stamp fabricated after molding against a flat silicon wafer, (b) Schematic of the stamp against a mold during the molding process, (c) Side view of the electrochemical imprinting of a silver film on a glass slide, (d) Image showing the results of multiple etch attempts using the S4 stamp on the silver film. Figure 2. Surface profiles of a 10 mm stamps molded against a polished silicon wafer at different (a) stamp holder temperatures and (b) compressive loads The profilometer data was taken with the glass surface facing upward (as shown in the insert image in (a)), so the curves shown in (a) represent concave surfaces. Ping-Ju Chen et al. / Procedia Manufacturing 34 (2019) 424â€“431 427 4 Author name / Procedia Manufacturing 00 (2019) 000â€“000 area with a diameter of 10 mm. The details of the etched line patterns are shown in Figure 3 (c) and (d) and an array of 200 nm hole patterns can also be produced using a 10 mm stamp shown in Figure 3 (e) and (f). 3. Plate-to-Roll pattering for scaling up S4 S4 can generate metallic nanopatterns with a stamp in a single step, and the scale-up of this process can be achieved by increasing the size (area) of the stamp. However, the cost of producing stamps with larger areas to the same tolerances is expected to go up exponentially with area [12]. An alternative is to consider a continuous process configuration such as a roll- to-roll process. Besides its obvious advantages from the standpoint of production rates, a roll-to-roll configuration reduces the contact between the stamp and film to a line contact, which is much easier to produce and maintain than a large-area contact. To investigate the feasibility of such a scheme, we designed an experimental setup to perform S4 patterning using a plate-to-roll (P2R) configuration. The primary purpose for this intermediate step is because it allows us to understand if we can reproduce electrochemical imprinting with a moving line contact without having to fabricate and patterns rollers with the glassy solid electrolyte. In this approach, a flexible substrate (e.g. polyimide film) coated with silver is wrapped around a metallic roller (constructed from a yoke style cam follower with a diameter of 31.75 mm and a width of 12.7 mm). This Ag clad tape is produced by depositing a 10 nm adhesion layer of chromium followed by a 2 Âµm silver layer on Kapton tape, using e-beam evaporation. A flat patterned (AgI)0.5-(AgPO3)0.5 stamp (made by the aforementioned molding process) is fed into the roller with a predefined pressure to produce good electrical contact between the patterned stamp and the silver film to be etched. The stamp is translated tangentially to the roller at a preprogrammed speed for a predetermined distance. The contact force between the stamp and the roller causes the roller to rotate so that we have a no-slip condition between the roller and the stamp. An electrolytic cell is created with the roller (and Ag film) as the anode and the stamp holder as the cathode to produce continuous electrochemical patterning of the silver film as it comes into contact with the stamp. In this approach, the actual contact area while etching is only a line (or a very thin rectangle) along which the roller makes contact with the substrate. Due to this smaller line contact area, the compressive load for patterning using the S4 process is greatly reduced compared to the regular S4 stamp-based imprinting process. Additionally, the electrochemical processes are also localized and easier to monitor and control. Figure 4. S4 patterning using a plate-to-roll scheme: (a) Schematic for implemeting S4 in a roll-to-plate scheme (b) Experimental setup (c) Etched silver film shows an initial line contact is obtained when etching with both the roller and the stamp are kept fixed under a compressive load of 1 lb. (d) Etched silver film from the 1st P2R attempt where the stamp is moving at a speed of 250 Âµm/sec while maintaining a line contact with the silver film. A 6 mm continuous etching of the silver film while rolling was established using constant voltage patterning at 2 V with a compressive load of 5 lb. (e) SEM image of the nanostructures patterned on the silver film of Figure 4 (d) and inset shows the details of the patterned silver film Figure 4 (a) and (b) show a schematic depiction of the process and a photograph of the plate-to-roll S4 patterning system. The roll-to-plate system is implemented on an Aerotech precision 3-axis positioning stage. The patterned stamp mounted on the table while roller is carried by a frame mounted on the z-axis of the stage via a load cell that monitors the contact load between the roller and the stamp. A user- written servo is used to control the contact load by moving the z-axis, while the x-axis of the stage executes the preprogrammed translation of the stamp. To check the feasibility of electrochemically patterning silver with a moving line contact due to the rolling motion of the roller, a 10 mm Figure 3. S4 Patterning of silver over a circular area with a diameter of 10 mm. (a) Image of the patterned S4 stamp (b) Image of multiple attempts of S4 patterning on silver film with a successful patterning attempt, (c) and (d) SEM images of line patterns of silver over a large area with a zoom-in image, (e) and (f) An array of hole patterns is produced using a 10 mm S4 stamp 428 Ping-Ju Chen et al. / Procedia Manufacturing 34 (2019) 424â€“431 Author name / Procedia Manufacturing 00 (2019) 000â€“000 5 stamp (whose fabrication was described in the previous section) is used. First, we check if we are able to establish line contact across the width of the stamp by keeping the roller and the stamp fixed while applying a load of 3 lb and running the electrolytic cell in a constant current mode. The etched area of silver (Figure 4 (c)) obtained using this approach suggests good line contact between the roller and the stamp. Electrochemical patterning was attempted by moving the stamp at a speed of 250 Âµm/sec for a distance of 6 mm while applying a compressive load of 3 lb and a constant voltage of 2 V to the system. Figure 4 (d) shows an optical image of a continuously patterned domain on the silver film. The SEM image in Figure 4 (e) shows the 320 nm diameter hole pattern array produced on the silver surface using this approach. Thus, we have demonstrated the feasibility of producing patterns with the S4 process using a moving line contact. The plate-to-roll system can be used to automatically produce repeated nanopatterned domains on a flexible film by repeating the aforementioned program of establishing servo-controlled contact load between the roller and the stamp and then translating the stamp through a fixed distance. This results in an interrupted continuous patterning process. 3.1. Contact-width Computation. The width of the line contact is an important parameter, because it determines how long a point on the Ag film is in contact with the stamp and undergoing etching. This is used to determine the current/voltage level for the process as well as the speed of translation of the stamp. The contact width can be calculated using Hertz theory: (1) where ð��¹ð��¹ is the applied force, ð��¸ð��¸#, ð��¸ð��¸$ are the elastic moduli of elasticity for cylinder 1 and 2 and ð�œˆð�œˆ# , ð�œˆð�œˆ$ are the Poissonâ€™s ratios, respectively. ð�‘™ð�‘™ is the length of contact and ð�‘‘ð�‘‘#, ð�‘‘ð�‘‘$ are the diameters of the cylinders 1 and 2. Figure 5 shows the case when calculating the contact area of a cylinder on a plate, ð�‘‘ð�‘‘$ â†’ âˆž. Figure 5. Contact between a cylinder and a plate Table 1. Material Properties of AgIAgPO3 and Kapton tape E n Kapton tape 0.05 GPa 0.33 (AgI)0.5(AgPO3)0.5 18.4 GPa 0.39 The calculated contact width is around 1.1 mm when applying a compressive force of 3 lb. on the roller wrapped with Kapton tape, which can also be seen in Figure 4 (c). 3.2. Experimental characterization of S4 rolling contact patterning: Activation voltage of (AgI)0.5-(AgPO3)0.5 In any electrochemical system, it is important to determine appropriate ranges of voltage and current density at which to operate the system. A voltage that is too high can lead to an undesirable side reaction that can destroy the solid electrolyte and one that is too low can fail to activate the mobile species in the electrolyte and produce no patterning. Cyclic voltammetry is a widely used technique for studying reactions in an electrochemical system to acquire qualitative information about electrode reactions. By measuring the current response as voltage is scanned, a voltage potential at which the current peaks and a workable current range can be obtained. These IV curves obtained from a voltammogram provide a good starting point for deciding the voltage needed to perform roll-patterning with S4, while the current range gives information necessary for constant-current patterning of S4. Linear sweep cyclic voltammetry was carried out for 6 cycles on a stationary stamp in contact with the roller (non-blocking). The voltage ranged from 0 to 4 V with a scan rate of 150 mV/s and a step size of 2.5mV, and the result can be seen in Figure 6. A peak potential of 0.22 V is shown in Figure 6 (b). This corresponds to the activation energy reported for (AgI)0.5-(AgPO3)0.5 of 0.2-0.25 eV [15]. As the potential reaches a peak potential, the current on the surface increases and exhibits a peak, but soon after the current response reaches a plateau of 500 uA between 0.2 V and 1 V. Since the current is a direct measure of the rate at which ions are being exchanged through the electrode-electrolyte interface, it indicates the rate at which the mobile species (Ag+) can diffuse from the AgIAgPO3 solid electrolyte to the electrode surface. Figure 6. Linear cyclic voltammograms for AgI-AgPO3 and silver: (a) is the full range voltammogram of 0 V â€“ 4 V and (b) is the zoom-in view for (a) at 0-1 V for better visualization 3.3. Master mold preparation The master mold used to mold molten glass into patterned stamps in this report is an array of 320 nm diameter holes in Nickel (shown in Figure 7). To facilitate demolding and minimize contamination of the mold surface with glass residue, the master mold surface is coated with a low surface energy material. Fluorinated trichlorosilanes provide a convenient route to low adhesion surfaces since they can be deposited via chemical vapour deposition and form a strong covalent bond b = 2FÏ€ l 1âˆ’Î½12( ) E1 + 1âˆ’Î½22( ) E2 1/ d1( )+ 1/ d2( ) âŽ¡ âŽ£ âŽ¢ âŽ¢ âŽ¤ âŽ¦ âŽ¥ âŽ¥ 1/2 Ping-Ju Chen et al. / Procedia Manufacturing 34 (2019) 424â€“431 429 6 Author name / Procedia Manufacturing 00 (2019) 000â€“000 with hydroxyl-terminated surfaces [16]. They are commonly used as mold release agents on silicon and silicon dioxide surfaces, where minimal non-stick coating thickness is desired. The cleaned nickel master is treated with FDTS (Perfluorodecyltrichlorosilane) using molecular vapor deposition. This anti-adhesion coating preserves an uncontaminated mold surface through several (around 10) molding/demolding cycles. After it is spent and contaminated with glass residue, it can be removed by reactive ion etching and a fresh coat can be applied to the mold surface. Figure 7. Images of Nickel master: (a) optical image of 320 nm hole array, (b) SEM image of the master 3.4. Calibration experiments with rolling contact patterning: constant voltage patterning Although the activation voltage of the solid electrolyte has been confirmed to be around 0.2 V, it is still not clear what applied voltage allows electrochemical etching of silver while rolling. A series of voltage control experiments is designed to find the voltage that is able to start the roll patterning of S4. The voltage is set above the activation voltage (~0.22 V) and below the secondary reaction taking place (~1 V) according to the linear potential sweep voltammogram. A 10 mm stamp is patterned (process developed in Section 2) using a master with an array of holes with diameters of 320 nm. The fabricated stamp is then scanned using AFM to obtain the surface topography before etching (shown in Figure 8). As shown, there is no missing pillars on a 7 Âµm x 7 Âµm sample scan area and the average height of pillars is about 125 nm after stamp making. Figure 8. A 10 mm stamp molded against the hole array master for R2P constant voltage patterning: (left) optical image of the 10 mm stamp, (middle) surface topography obtained using AFM and (right) average height of the pillars after stamp making The calibration experiment was then carried out at constant voltage with continuous roll-patterning starting from 0.3 V to 0.7 V with 0.1 V increments. Initially an imprinting load of 1 lbf was used. It was found that, with this low contact load, patterns could not be generated due to the poor physical contact between the silver film and stamp surface from intrinsic surface flatness. A constant imprinting load of 3lbf was found to be adequate. Figure 9 shows SEM and AFM images of the generated pattern geometry under different voltages with a constant rolling speed at 50 Âµm/s and a constant compressive load of 3 lb. Roll-patterning was performed for a distance of 6 mm for each case. Figure 9. SEM images of patterned silver for constant voltage patterning of S4 at (a) 0.3 V, (b) 0.5 V and (c) 0.7 V under 3 lb. compressive force and constant rolling speed 50 Âµm/s, (d), (e) and (f) are the AFM scans to obtain the geometries of etched silver films for 0.3 V, 0.5 V and 0.7 V respectively Voltages of 0.3 V and 0.4 V (image not shown) produced incomplete patterns as the etching only localized on the high- points of the silver films. Voltages of 0.5 V to 0.7 V (Figure 9 (b) and (c)) were able to generate uniform patterns and reproduced the geometry of the stamp as the depths and diameters of the etched holes are similar to the geometries of the molded stamp (Figure 8 (right)). By comparing the AFM of a new molded stamp and the etched/patterned silver, it was determined that 0.7 V produces the best patterning result with the chosen rolling speed of 50 Âµm/s. However, during the constant-voltage patterning experiments, large current fluctuations (possibly due to changing contact area between the stamp and the silver film) were noticed. This is an indication that the etch rate is not a constant and would be challenging to predict and estimate the correct speed of rolling contact patterning to result in the desired depth of patterning. Therefore, there is a need to conduct calibration experiments for this process with a constant patterning/etching current. 3.5. Calibration experiments with rolling contact patterning: constant current patterning Since this electrolyte is a pure ionic conductor, the total charge and mass transported across the interface are expected to be proportional, once a steady process is established. Integration of the current profile for the chronoamperometry curve yields the total charge for each print. Further, the total charge required can be computed from the area and depth of patterning of the patterned area. If it is possible to perform P2R patterning using constant current supply, then the chronoamperometry data will provide a very accurate indication of the etching progression. Based on the voltammogram from the previous section, the current reaches a plateau of 500 ÂµA after activation peak current, therefore the constant-current patterning is performed in the interval of 300 ÂµA to 1000 ÂµA with 100 ÂµA increments. To compare the etched patterns, roll-patterning is performed for a distance of 6 mm for each current level with a 3 lbf imprinting load and a speed of 50 Âµm/s. The patterned silver film is shown in Figure 10. 430 Ping-Ju Chen et al. / Procedia Manufacturing 34 (2019) 424â€“431 Author name / Procedia Manufacturing 00 (2019) 000â€“000 7 Figure 10. Optical image of constant current patterning from 300 ÂµA to 1000 ÂµA As one can see in Figure 11 (a), etching at 300 ÂµA does not generate uniform patterns as the etched holes have different depths from the silver surface. When current increases to 400 â€“ 500 ÂµA (Figure 11 (b) and (c)), silver etching gets more uniform across a 10 mm x 6 mm area. However, the rolling speed of 50 Âµm/s is too fast for these etching currents, the metal moves across the contact area with the stamp too fast to permit etching to the desired depth. This leaves a rough, unetched silver surface with unevenly etched holes. Patterning at 600 ÂµA, (as shown in Figure 11 (d)) with a rolling speed 50 Âµm/s reproduces a stamp pattern with a very high degree of fidelity. Even, manufacturing defects on the master mold (small deposits around the lips of the holes) are patterned into the silver film. This is shown in the inserts next to Figure 11 (d). When the patterning current is larger than 600 ÂµA, a fast etch rate with a slow rolling speed distorts the patterns. This is shown in Figure 11 (e) and (f). Figure 11. SEM images of etched silver film under constant current patterning at (a) 300 ÂµA â€“ (f) 800 ÂµA (scale bar: 5 um), and (d) subset shows the comparison between the master mold and the etched silver for 600 ÂµA 3.6. Calibration experiments with rolling contact patterning: rolling speed To optimize the rolling speed for each level of patterning current, P2R patterning was conducted with the rolling speed varied from 10 Âµm/s to 50 Âµm/s. The patterning experiments were repeated for current levels ranging from 200 ÂµA to 500 ÂµA. In these experiments, the roll-patterning was conducted for a 6 mm distance with the application of a 3 lbf imprinting load to maintain contact. The optimal rolling speed for each patterning current level along with the patterns produced is shown in Figure 12. The total charge for silver removal for each stamped region shown in Figure 12 can be calculated from the contact area divided by the rolling speed and multiplied by the patterning current. It is interesting to note that in all these cases, the total charge stayed almost constant, around 13 mC to 15 mC. This indicates that roughly the same amount of silver is removed in each of these runs. Figure 12. SEM images of optimal rolling speed for each constant current patterning for (a) 500 ÂµA, (b) 400 ÂµA, (c) 300 ÂµA and (d) 200 ÂµA (scale bar: 4um) 4. Conclusions S4 is a facile and scalable manufacturing process for directly patterning large areas of Ag films with arbitrary nanostructures. It is inexpensive compared to other indirect nanopatterning techniques. Patterning of Ag nanostructures over a circular area with a diameter of 10 mm has been demonstrated. The potential of using S4 as a continuous patterning process has also been demonstrated by Plate-to-Roll (P2R) patterning 10x6 mm2 domains of Ag films with 200 nm hole or pillar arrays having an aspect ratio of 2. P2R patterning with S4 was demonstrated for both constant voltage and constant current patterning. Optimal process parameters (voltage, current and roll-speed) were obtained. Based on this work, some degradation of the glass stamp was observed, but the exact cause will be looked at in future work as well as wear rates of the stamp over time. Our studies will continue with the theoretical modeling of the process to understand and characterize the phenomena (ion transport in solid electrolytes) that underlie this process. We also plan to extend this process to additional metals, specifically copper, because of its widespread use in electronics. Acknowledgements The authors acknowledge the contributions of Kyle Jacobs who introduced the use of AgI-AgPO3 glasses in the S4 process. Haenah Kim and Pierce R. Blazina helped with producing the glass for the experiments reported. This research was conducted in the Micro and Nano Mechanical Systems Laboratory and the Materials Research Laboratory at the University of Illinois. The assistance received from staff of these labs is acknowledge. References [1] M. J. Madou, Solid-State Physics, Fluidics, and Analytical Techniques in Micro- and Nanotechnology, vol. 94, no. 2. Tseng, â€œRecent developments in micromilling using focused ion beam technology,â€� J. Micromechanics Microengineering, vol. 14, no. 4, pp. R15â€“R34, Apr. 2004. [4] K. Felmet, Y.-L. Loo, and Y. Sun, â€œPatterning conductive copper by nanotransfer printing,â€� Appl. Phys. Lett., vol. 85, no. 15, pp. 3316â€“3318, Oct. 2004. [5] J. C. Hulteen, D. A. Treichel, M. T. Smith, M. L. Duval, T. R. Jensen, and R. P. Van Duyne, â€œNanosphere Lithography: Size- Tunable Silver Nanoparticle and Surface Cluster Arrays,â€� J. Phys. Chem. B, vol. 103, no. 19, pp. 3854â€“3863, May 1999. [6] B. PÃ¤ivÃ¤nranta, A. Langner, E. Kirk, C. David, and Y. Ekinci, â€œSub-10 nm patterning using EUV interference lithography,â€� Nanotechnology, vol. 22, no. 37, p. 375302, Sep. 2011. [7] A. Kumar, K. H. Hsu, K. E. Jacobs, P. M. Ferreira, and N. X. Fang, â€œDirect metal nano-imprinting using an embossed solid electrolyte stamp,â€� Nanotechnology, vol. 22, no. 15, 2011. [8] K. H. Hsu, P. L. Schultz, P. M. Ferreira, and N. X. Fang, â€œElectrochemical Nanoimprinting with Solid-State Superionic Stamps,â€� Nano Lett., vol. 7, no. 2, pp. 446â€“451, Feb. 2007. [9] P. L. Schultz, K. H. Hsu, N. X. Fang, and P. M. Ferreira, â€œSolid- state electrochemical nanoimprinting of copper,â€� J. Vac. Sci. Technol. B Microelectron. Nanom. Struct., vol. 25, no. 6, p. 2419, 2007. [10] K. H. Hsu, P. L. Schultz, P. M. Ferreira, and N. X. Fang, â€œExploiting transport of guest metal ions in a host ionic crystal lattice for nanofabrication: Cu nanopatterning with Ag2S,â€� Appl. Phys. A, vol. 97, no. 4, pp. 863â€“868, Dec. 2009. [11] K. E. Jacobs et al., â€œSolid-state superionic stamping with silver iodideâ€“silver metaphosphate glass,â€� Nanotechnology, vol. 22, no. 42, p. 425301, Oct. 2011. [12] H. Rickert, Electrochemistry of Solids. Berlin, Heidelbergâ€‰: Springer Berlin Heidelberg, 1982. [13] K. E. Jacobs and P. M. Ferreira, â€œPainting and Direct Writing of Silver Nanostructures on Phosphate Glass with Electron Beam Irradiation,â€� Adv. Funct. Mater., vol. 25, no. 33, pp. 5261â€“5268, 2015. [14] K. Jacobs, â€œSOLID-STATE SUPERIONIC CONDUCTORS FOR ADVANCED NANOSCALE FABRICATION,â€� University of Illinois at Urbana-Champaign, 2017. [15] C. B. Bragatto, A. C. M. Rodrigues, and J.-L. Souquet, â€œDissociation Equilibrium and Charge Carrier Formation in AgIâ€“ AgPO 3 Glasses,â€� J. Phys. Chem. C, vol. 121, no. 25, pp. 13507â€“ 13514, Jun. 2017. [16] J. B. Brzoska, I. Ben Azouz, and F. Rondelez, â€œSilanization of Solid Substrates: A Step toward Reproducibility,â€� Langmuir, vol. 10, no. 11, pp. 4367â€“4373, 1994. age, current and ro

head.ce:title: An Experimental Investigation into Plate-to-Roll Patterning with Solid-State Superionic Stamping

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: University of Illinois at Urbana-Champaign, 1206 W Green St, Urbana, IL and 61801, USA

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Metallic nanostructures play an important role in micro and nanotechnology, from interconnects in electronics, electrodes in chemical sensors, batteries, plasmonic waveguides, structural color generation in sub-wavelength optics and catalysts in micro-reactor surfaces. Silver, of all the elements, possesses the highest electrical conductivity and (as a result) unique interaction with visible light when patterned at the nanoscale, making it ideal for electronics, optics and chemical sensing applications. Solid-State Superionic Stamping (S4) is an electrochemical nano-imprinting process that is capable of directly patterning silver nanostructures. In this paper, we demonstrate a simple method to scale up S4 patterning of silver using a plate-to-roll format to explore the potential of the S4 technology in a roll-to-roll configuration for high-volume manufacturing. In this format, a roller with a silver-coated polymer film wrapped around it is rolled without slip over a planar, patterned (x)AgI(1-x)AgPO3, x=0.5, stamp while creating an electrochemical cell with the silver film as the anode. This format allows us to explore the advantages of patterning with a moving line contact (as opposed to the large stationary surface contact in the imprinting/stamping format) between the stamp and roller, but without the added complexity of having to make a patterned electrolyte roller. We demonstrate the making of 6x10 mm2 patterned silver surfaces with arrays of 200 nm holes or pillars having an aspect ratio of up to 2. The effects of process parameters including rolling speed, contact pressure, patterning voltage and current on the pattern quality and the stamp surface degradation are studied.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: K. Jacobs, “SOLID-STATE SUPERIONIC CONDUCTORS FOR ADVANCED NANOSCALE FABRICATION,” University of Illinois at Urbana-Champaign, 2017.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Dissociation Equilibrium and Charge Carrier Formation in AgI–AgPO 3 Glasses
J. Phys. Chem. C
Silanization of Solid Substrates: A Step toward Reproducibility
Langmuir
